exercises.example_sentence: このボタンを押すと、機械が動きます。
exercises.translation: When you press this button, the machine operates.
wrong_choice_explanations: たら means 'if/when (completed condition)' — it could work but と is more natural for describing automatic, predictable results.
ば means 'if' but is more formal and literary — it doesn't fit as naturally in this practical instruction context.
なら means 'if (given that)' and is used for assumptions — it doesn't fit here because we're describing a direct cause-and-effect relationship.
exercises.hiragana_full: このぼたんをおすと、きかいがうごきます。

audio_alternatives.kanji: このボタンを押せば、機械が動きます。
audio_alternatives.hiragana_full: このぼたんをおせば、きかいがうごきます。

audio_alternatives.kanji: このボタンを押したら、機械が動きます。
audio_alternatives.hiragana_full: このぼたんをおしたら、きかいがうごきます。

exercises.example_sentence: 春になると、桜が咲きます。
exercises.translation: When spring comes, cherry blossoms bloom.
wrong_choice_explanations: たら means 'if/when (completed condition)' — it could work but と is more natural for describing natural, seasonal cycles.
ても means 'even if' and expresses contrast — it doesn't fit here because spring naturally brings cherry blossoms.
から means 'because/since' and expresses cause — it doesn't fit here because we're describing a temporal sequence, not a causal explanation.
exercises.hiragana_full: はるになると、さくらがさきます。

audio_alternatives.kanji: 春になれば、桜が咲きます。
audio_alternatives.hiragana_full: はるになれば、さくらがさきます。

audio_alternatives.kanji: 春が来ると、桜が咲きます。
audio_alternatives.hiragana_full: はるがくると、さくらがさきます。

exercises.example_sentence: 電気を消すと、部屋が暗くなります。
exercises.translation: When you turn off the lights, the room becomes dark.
wrong_choice_explanations: たら means 'if/when (completed condition)' — it could work but と is more natural for describing automatic, predictable results.
ば means 'if' but is more formal — it doesn't fit as naturally when describing a simple cause-and-effect relationship.
ても means 'even if' and expresses contrast — it doesn't fit here because turning off lights naturally makes rooms dark.
exercises.hiragana_full: でんきをけすと、へやがくらくなります。

audio_alternatives.kanji: 電気を消したら、部屋が暗くなります。
audio_alternatives.hiragana_full: でんきをけしたら、へやがくらくなります。

audio_alternatives.kanji: 電気を消すと、部屋が暗くなる。
audio_alternatives.hiragana_full: でんきをけすと、へやがくらくなる。

exercises.example_sentence: 朝起きると、まず顔を洗います。
exercises.translation: When I wake up in the morning, I first wash my face.
wrong_choice_explanations: たら means 'if/when (completed condition)' — it could work but と is more natural for describing habitual sequences.
ながら means 'while doing' — it doesn't fit here because waking up and washing face are sequential, not simultaneous actions.
でも means 'but/however' and expresses contrast — it doesn't fit here because washing your face is a natural follow-up to waking up.
exercises.hiragana_full: あさおきると、まずかおをあらいます。

audio_alternatives.kanji: 朝起きたら、まず顔を洗います。
audio_alternatives.hiragana_full: あさおきたら、まずかおをあらいます。

audio_alternatives.kanji: 朝起きると、最初に顔を洗います。
audio_alternatives.hiragana_full: あさおきると、さいしょにかおをあらいます。

exercises.example_sentence: 雨が降ると、道が濡れます。
exercises.translation: When it rains, the roads get wet.
wrong_choice_explanations: のに means 'although/despite' and expresses contrast — it doesn't fit here because roads getting wet when it rains is a natural consequence, not a contradiction.
から means 'because/since' and expresses cause — it doesn't fit here because we're describing a natural sequence, not explaining causation.
ので means 'because/since' and expresses cause — it doesn't fit here because we're describing what happens when it rains, not why roads get wet.
exercises.hiragana_full: あめがふると、みちがぬれます。

audio_alternatives.kanji: 雨が降れば、道が濡れます。
audio_alternatives.hiragana_full: あめがふれば、みちがぬれます。

audio_alternatives.kanji: 雨が降ったら、道が濡れます。
audio_alternatives.hiragana_full: あめがふったら、みちがぬれます。

exercises.example_sentence: 12時になると、チャイムが鳴ります。
exercises.translation: When it becomes 12 o'clock, the chime rings.
wrong_choice_explanations: まで means 'until' — it doesn't fit here because we're describing what happens at 12 o'clock, not what happens up until that time.
ば means 'if' but is more formal — it doesn't fit as naturally when describing regular, automatic occurrences.
なら means 'if (given that)' and is used for assumptions — it doesn't fit here because we're describing a regular schedule, not making assumptions.
exercises.hiragana_full: じゅうにじになると、チャイムがなります。

audio_alternatives.kanji: 12時になったら、チャイムが鳴ります。
audio_alternatives.hiragana_full: じゅうにじになったら、チャイムがなります。

audio_alternatives.kanji: 12時になれば、チャイムが鳴ります。
audio_alternatives.hiragana_full: じゅうにじになれば、チャイムがなります。

exercises.example_sentence: この薬を飲むと、眠くなります。
exercises.translation: When you take this medicine, you become sleepy.
wrong_choice_explanations: けれど means 'but/however' and expresses contrast — it doesn't fit here because becoming sleepy is an expected effect, not a contradiction.
ても means 'even if' and expresses contrast — it doesn't fit here because becoming sleepy is an expected effect of the medicine.
から means 'because/since' and expresses cause — it doesn't fit here because we're describing what happens when you take medicine, not explaining why.
exercises.hiragana_full: このくすりをのむと、ねむくなります。

audio_alternatives.kanji: この薬を飲んだら、眠くなります。
audio_alternatives.hiragana_full: このくすりをのんだら、ねむくなります。

audio_alternatives.kanji: この薬を飲むと、眠くなる。
audio_alternatives.hiragana_full: このくすりをのむと、ねむくなる。

exercises.example_sentence: お湯を沸かすと、湯気が出ます。
exercises.translation: When you boil water, steam comes out.
wrong_choice_explanations: 間に means 'during/while' — it doesn't fit here because we're describing the natural result of boiling water, not something that happens during the process.
ながら means 'while doing' — it doesn't fit here because we're describing the result of boiling, not simultaneous actions.
ば means 'if' but is more formal — it doesn't fit as naturally when describing natural, physical processes.
exercises.hiragana_full: おゆをわかすと、ゆげがでます。

audio_alternatives.kanji: 水を沸騰させると、湯気が出ます。
audio_alternatives.hiragana_full: みずをふっとうさせると、ゆげがでます。

audio_alternatives.kanji: お湯を沸かしたら、湯気が出ます。
audio_alternatives.hiragana_full: おゆをわかしたら、ゆげがでます。

exercises.example_sentence: 右に曲がると、駅が見えます。
exercises.translation: When you turn right, you can see the station.
wrong_choice_explanations: のに means 'although/despite' and expresses contrast — it doesn't fit here because seeing the station is the expected result of turning right.
ば means 'if' but is more formal — it doesn't fit as naturally in casual direction-giving contexts.
なら means 'if (given that)' and is used for assumptions — it doesn't fit here because we're describing what you'll see when turning, not making assumptions.
exercises.hiragana_full: みぎにまがると、えきがみえます。

audio_alternatives.kanji: 右に曲がれば、駅が見えます。
audio_alternatives.hiragana_full: みぎにまがれば、えきがみえます。

audio_alternatives.kanji: 右に曲がったら、駅が見えます。
audio_alternatives.hiragana_full: みぎにまがったら、えきがみえます。

exercises.example_sentence: 冬になると、雪が降ります。
wrong_choice_explanations: まで means 'until' — it doesn't fit here because we're describing what happens in winter, not what happens until winter arrives.
から means 'because/since' and expresses cause — it doesn't fit here because we're describing what happens in winter, not explaining why it snows.
ので means 'because/since' and expresses cause — it doesn't fit here because we're describing a natural seasonal occurrence, not providing causal explanation.
exercises.hiragana_full: ふゆになると、ゆきがふります。

audio_alternatives.kanji: 冬になれば、雪が降ります。
audio_alternatives.hiragana_full: ふゆになれば、ゆきがふります。

audio_alternatives.kanji: 冬になったら、雪が降ります。
audio_alternatives.hiragana_full: ふゆになったら、ゆきがふります。
